difficulty_factors: Silk is a protein fiber — sensitive to alkaline agents, mechanical agitation, and water spotting
Oil spreads via capillary action on silk's smooth filament structure
Dry cleaning preferred — wet treatment risks water marks and dye bleeding

diy.title: Cooking Oil on Silk
mixSteps: Mix 1 drop of Dawn in 1 cup of cool water — silk cannot handle concentrated detergent
Have white vinegar ready as a rinse (1 tbsp per cup cool water)

steps.instruction: Blot — don't rub — any wet oil with a clean white cloth. Silk fiber is weakened 20% when wet, so no friction.
steps.why: Rubbing silk when wet damages the fiber surface and can cause permanent texture change.

steps.instruction: Cover the stain with cornstarch or talcum powder. Let it sit 15-20 minutes undisturbed.
steps.why: The powder adsorbs surface oil before it wicks deeper along silk's smooth filaments.

steps.instruction: Gently brush off the powder. If the stain is noticeably lighter, repeat the powder step.
steps.why: Multiple powder applications can remove significant oil without any wet chemistry — safest approach for silk.

steps.instruction: Dab the dilute soap solution onto the stain with a white cloth. Press gently — never rub.
steps.why: The surfactant emulsifies remaining oil so water can carry it away.

steps.instruction: Rinse by dabbing with cool water. Follow immediately with the vinegar rinse to restore silk's acidic pH.
steps.why: Silk is a protein fiber with a natural pH of 4-6. Restoring acidity prevents dulling and fiber damage.

steps.instruction: Lay flat on a clean towel to air dry — never wring, twist, or use heat.
steps.why: Heat sets oil stains. Wringing damages silk fiber permanently.

diy.ifStillThere: Take it to a professional dry cleaner — tell them it's cooking oil on silk and ask for POG pre-spotting before the cleaning cycle. Silk responds beautifully to proper dry cleaning. Hydrocarbon or GreenEarth solvent is ideal for silk.
proTips: The powder step is your best friend on silk — it removes oil with zero chemical risk.
Never use hot water on silk oil stains. Hot water sets oil AND can cause water spotting on silk.
Avoid rubbing alcohol — it can strip silk dyes and leave permanent marks.
diy.safety: Always test any solution on a hidden seam first. Silk dyes bleed easily — if you see color on your cloth, stop immediately and take to a professional. Never use chlorine bleach, ammonia, or enzyme products on silk — silk IS a protein fiber and these agents damage it.
diy.whyThisWorks: Cooking oil on silk is a triglyceride (fat molecule) sitting on a smooth protein filament. The oil spreads fast on silk because the filament surface is so smooth — capillary action pulls it along the threads. Cornstarch absorbs the oil physically. The trace amount of dish soap emulsifies what's left, and the vinegar rinse restores silk's natural acidic environment.

home.title: Cooking Oil on Silk — Careful Removal
home.confidenceNote: Silk is delicate. Fresh stains respond well to powder absorption. Aged oil stains on silk often need professional dry cleaning.

primaryProduct.name: Carbona Stain Devils #5 (Fat & Cooking Oil)
primaryProduct.why: Solvent-based formula designed for oil stains — safer for silk than water-heavy methods
primaryProduct.amazonSearchQuery: Carbona Stain Devils fat cooking oil

alternativeProducts.name: Dawn Ultra Dish Soap (Blue)
alternativeProducts.why: Mild surfactant emulsifies oil — use extremely diluted (1 drop per cup water) on silk

alternativeProducts.name: The Laundress Stain Solution
alternativeProducts.why: Formulated for delicate fabrics including silk — plant-based surfactants

home.prepStep: Blot excess oil gently — do NOT rub. Apply cornstarch or talcum powder and let absorb 15-20 minutes before any wet treatment.

method.instruction: Blot excess oil with a clean white cloth. Apply cornstarch or talcum powder generously and wait 15-20 minutes.
method.why: Absorbs surface oil without risking silk's delicate structure.

method.instruction: Brush off powder. Apply Carbona Stain Devils #5 per label — test on hidden area first.
method.why: Solvent-based formula dissolves oil without the water that causes silk spotting.

method.instruction: Blot treated area gently with a clean white cloth. Do not rinse with water unless product directs.
method.why: Solvent evaporates and carries dissolved oil with it.

method.instruction: If any residue remains and garment is washable, hand wash in cool water with 1 drop of mild soap. Rinse immediately. Add 1 tbsp white vinegar to rinse water.
method.why: Brief hand wash removes residual oil. Vinegar restores silk's natural acidic pH and luster.

method.instruction: Lay flat on a clean towel. Roll gently to extract moisture. Air dry — never wring or use heat.
method.why: Heat sets any remaining oil. Wringing permanently damages silk fiber structure.

materialWarnings.warning: Silk weakens 20% when wet — handle with extreme care. No rubbing, no wringing, no heat.
Never use chlorine bleach, enzyme products, or ammonia on silk — silk IS protein and these agents digest/damage it.
Water can leave permanent spots on silk. Minimize wet contact time. Use distilled water when possible.

ifItDoesntWork.escalation: If the oil stain persists after two careful attempts, take to a professional dry cleaner. Specify cooking oil on silk. Ask for GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent — both are excellent for silk and dissolve cooking oil effectively.

proTips: Cornstarch is the safest first step for oil on silk — zero chemical risk.
Never apply water to a fresh oil stain on silk — it causes water spotting and spreads the oil.
Silk responds beautifully to proper dry cleaning — don't hesitate to go professional.
home.whyThisWorks: Oil is non-polar; silk is a smooth protein filament. Absorbent powder captures oil physically. A mild solvent or surfactant emulsifies the rest. The key on silk is minimal wet contact and zero heat.
home.timeEstimate: 20-30 minutes (may require professional follow-up for aged stains)

pro.title: Cooking Oil on Silk — Professional Dry-Side Protocol
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (lipid-based triglyceride). Cooking oils are triglycerides of C16-C18 fatty acids — olive oil is primarily oleic acid (C18:1), vegetable oils contain linoleic (C18:2). These unsaturated fatty acids oxidize over time, polymerizing into varnish-like films that resist solvent removal. On silk (fibroin protein fiber, pH 4-6, smooth filament surface), oil spreads rapidly via capillary action along the smooth filaments. Silk's natural sericin coating may already be partially removed by prior cleaning, exposing the fibroin to direct oil penetration. Treatment approach: dry-side POG pre-spot to dissolve lipid, followed by gentle GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent cycle. NEVER wet-side first on silk — water spotting is a greater risk than the oil stain itself.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG or Pyratex® POG
spottingProtocol.technique: Dry-side POG pre-spot — dissolve triglyceride before cleaning cycle
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place silk face-down on a clean white towel on the spotting board. Apply POG to the back of the oil stain with a dauber — controlled application, not flooding. Silk is smooth and thin, so solvent penetrates quickly. Tamp gently with the bone spatula — light taps, NOT rubbing. Silk fiber surface is easily abraded when wet with solvent. You'll see oil transferring to the towel below. Move to a fresh towel position and repeat. For silk, 2-3 light rounds of POG beat one heavy application — you're avoiding solvent pooling that can cause ring marks. Dan Eisen's principle: always wet the area with VDS or solvent before applying POG to prevent ring formation on delicates.

spottingProtocol.technique: Edge feathering — prevent solvent ring on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: After POG treatment, feather the edges with VDS (Formula 83). Apply VDS in a circle around the treated area, working outward to blend the edge. On silk, solvent rings are a real risk — the smooth filament surface makes any boundary visible. The VDS evaporates faster than POG, carrying any residue outward and preventing a hard edge. This step separates professional spotting from amateur work on silk.

spottingProtocol.agent: Machine cleaning — GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent preferred
spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle solvent cycle for complete oil removal
spottingProtocol.instruction: Load for GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent cycle — both rated A (excellent) for silk. GreenEarth is ideal: no odor retention, preserves hand-feel and luster. Hydrocarbon is also excellent for silk. PERC is acceptable but rated C — may leave odor and affect hand-feel. If using PERC, ensure complete solvent recovery. The POG pre-spot already dissolved the bulk oil, so the cleaning cycle handles residual oil and provides uniform finishing. Use silk-safe detergent charge.

spottingProtocol.technique: Transmitted light check for residual oil halo
spottingProtocol.instruction: After cleaning, hold silk up to transmitted light. Oil residue on silk shows as a darker, slightly translucent area against the surrounding fabric — visible only in transmitted light. If a halo persists, repeat POG pre-spot and re-clean. Do not press or finish until oil is confirmed removed — heat from pressing will oxidize and permanently set any remaining oil.

pro.escalation: If cooking oil has oxidized (yellowed, aged weeks+): POG alone may not suffice. Escalate to amyl acetate for stronger solvency on polymerized oil — safe on silk. For extreme cases, consider lipase enzyme in very brief application (2-3 minutes max, cool water, immediate rinse) — but this carries risk on silk since silk is protein. Professional judgment required.
pro.customerExplanation: We pre-spotted the cooking oil with a professional solvent that dissolves grease without touching your silk's color or texture, then ran it through our gentlest cleaning cycle. Silk responds beautifully to professional care — the oil is gone and the fabric's natural luster is preserved.
commonMistakes: Using water-based treatment first — water spots on silk are sometimes harder to remove than the original oil stain
Heavy mechanical action — silk abrades easily when wet with solvent. Light tapping only.
Skipping the VDS feathering step — leaves a visible solvent ring on silk's smooth surface
Using protease enzyme — silk IS protein. Protease digests silk fiber. NEVER use on silk.
pro.neutralization: No acid neutralization needed — this is a purely dry-side treatment. If any wet-side work was performed, restore pH with dilute acetic acid (1:10 GAA) before finishing.
pro.fabricRisks: Silk risks: dye bleeding (test first), water spotting, seam slippage from mechanical stress, fiber abrasion from aggressive tamping. GreenEarth is the safest solvent choice. Monitor for dye migration during cleaning — silk dyes are notoriously variable in fastness.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG Remover
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson NovaBone POG remover professional spotting
professional.note: Primary POG agent for cooking oil. Apply on dry side before cleaning cycle. Dissolves triglycerides on contact.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Formula 83 VDS
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson Formula 83 volatile dry solvent
professional.note: Essential for edge feathering on silk. Prevents solvent ring marks.

professional.name: Pyratex® POG
professional.amazonSearchQuery: Pyratex POG paint oil grease remover
professional.note: Alternative POG agent — volatile hydrocarbon blend with rapid evaporation.

household.name: Carbona Stain Devils #5 (Fat & Cooking Oil)
household.amazonSearchQuery: Carbona Stain Devils fat cooking oil
household.note: Consumer-grade dry solvent. Better than dish soap for silk because it minimizes water contact.

household.name: Dawn Ultra Dish Soap (Blue)
household.note: Use extremely diluted — 1 drop per cup of cool water. Last resort for silk. Follow with vinegar rinse.

household.name: Cornstarch
household.note: Safest first step for oil on silk. Apply generously, wait 15-20 min, brush off. Zero chemical risk.

title: Cooking Oil on Silk
stainChemistry: Cooking oil is a triglyceride — three fatty acid chains attached to a glycerol backbone. On silk, these non-polar molecules spread rapidly along the smooth protein filaments via capillary action, often forming a T-shaped or cross-shaped stain pattern as Dan Eisen described. Silk's smooth fibroin surface offers little resistance to oil penetration, but the good news is that same smoothness means oil sits on the surface longer than it does on cotton. The key threat is oxidation — unsaturated fatty acids in vegetable oils polymerize with exposure to air and heat, eventually forming a varnish-like film that resists all solvents. Fresh oil on silk is straightforward. Aged, oxidized oil on silk is a serious challenge.
whyThisWorks: POG solvent dissolves the triglyceride through like-dissolves-like chemistry — the nonpolar solvent molecules intercalate between the fatty acid chains and break the van der Waals forces holding the oil to the silk fiber. The dry-side approach keeps water away from silk, preventing the water spotting that plagues silk treatment. GreenEarth or hydrocarbon cleaning completes the job without the risks that PERC poses to silk (odor retention, hand-feel changes).

spottingProtocol.technique: Physical oil absorption — safest first step for silk
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cornstarch, talcum powder, or Fuller's earth; soft brush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply absorbent powder generously over the oil stain. On silk, this is always the first move — it removes oil with zero chemical risk. Let it sit undisturbed for 15-20 minutes. The powder will darken as it adsorbs the oil. Brush off gently with a soft brush. If the stain is noticeably lighter, repeat. On fresh cooking oil, two rounds of powder can remove 60-80% of the stain.

spottingProtocol.technique: Dry-side solvent dissolution of remaining triglyceride
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent, spotting board, clean white towels, bone spatula
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place silk face-down on a clean white towel. Apply POG to the back of the stain with a dauber — controlled, not flooding. Tamp gently with the flat of the bone spatula. Watch the towel below — oil will transfer as a darkened area. Move to a fresh towel position and repeat. On silk, use light rounds rather than one heavy application. After POG, feather edges with VDS (Formula 83) to prevent ring formation on silk's smooth surface.

spottingProtocol.agent: GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent cleaning cycle
spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle machine cleaning to complete oil removal
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dry cleaning machine with GreenEarth or HC solvent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Run through GreenEarth or hydrocarbon cycle with silk-safe detergent. Both solvents are rated A (excellent) for silk — no odor, no hand-feel change, excellent oil dissolution. The POG pre-spot handled the concentrated stain; the cleaning cycle removes residual oil uniformly. Avoid over-extraction — silk is sensitive to mechanical stress.

spottingProtocol.technique: Visual confirmation of complete oil removal
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hold silk up to transmitted light — oil residue shows as a translucent dark area. If clear, proceed to finishing. If residual halo visible, repeat POG pre-spot and re-clean. Never press until oil is confirmed removed — iron heat oxidizes remaining oil into permanent yellow discoloration on silk.

homeSolutions: Cornstarch or talcum powder: Apply generously to the oil stain, let absorb 15-20 minutes, brush off. Repeat 2-3 times. On fresh cooking oil on silk, this alone can handle most of the stain — zero risk to the fabric.
Carbona Stain Devils #5: Apply per label to the oil stain. Solvent-based so it minimizes water contact on silk. Test on a hidden seam first for colorfastness.

Extremely dilute Dawn (1 drop per cup cool water): Dab onto the stain gently — never rub. Rinse with cool water immediately. Follow with a dilute vinegar rinse (1 tbsp per cup water) to restore silk pH. Lay flat to dry.
Dry cleaner — for silk, professional dry cleaning is often the best and safest option. GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent dissolves cooking oil perfectly and is excellent for silk.
materialWarnings: NEVER use hot water on oil-stained silk — heat sets oil AND causes silk fiber damage.
NEVER use chlorine bleach, ammonia, or protease enzyme on silk — silk is a protein fiber and these agents damage it directly.
NEVER rub or wring silk — fiber weakens 20% when wet. Dab and press only.
Water spotting is a real risk on silk — minimize wet contact time and use distilled water.
Test ALL agents on a hidden seam — silk dye fastness varies dramatically between garments.
commonMistakes: Applying water first — on silk, water before solvent causes spotting AND spreads the oil. Always start dry-side.
Rubbing the stain — silk fiber abrades easily, especially when wet. Press and dab only.
Using enzyme detergent — protease enzymes attack silk's protein structure. Use only neutral or acidic agents.
Pressing before oil is fully removed — iron heat permanently yellows any residual cooking oil on silk.
scienceNote: Silk fibroin is a semicrystalline protein with a smooth, low-friction surface. Cooking oils spread faster on silk than on cotton because the smooth filament surface offers less resistance to capillary flow. However, that same smoothness means the oil doesn't penetrate as deeply into the fiber structure — it sits more superficially. This is why absorbent powder is so effective on fresh oil stains on silk: the oil is accessible on the surface. Dan Eisen's observation that oil stains form a cross or T pattern (following the weave) is particularly visible on silk due to the fabric's smooth hand and light reflection.
customerExplanation: Cooking oil on silk requires extra care because silk is a protein fiber — many cleaning agents that work on cotton can actually damage silk. We treated the oil with a professional solvent designed specifically for grease, using techniques that protect your silk's color and texture. The oil came out cleanly.
escalation: If the cooking oil has oxidized on silk (weeks old, yellowed), escalate to amyl acetate for stronger solvency. If still resistant, brief lipase enzyme application (2-3 minutes max, cool water, immediate rinse) is possible but carries risk on silk — professional judgment required. For irreplaceable silk garments, consider a specialist silk cleaner.
callPro: Call a professional when: the oil stain is more than a few days old; you see dye bleeding or water spotting during home treatment; the silk garment is labeled dry clean only; the item is high-value or irreplaceable. Silk responds beautifully to professional dry cleaning — this is one case where the pro route is worth every penny.
solventNote: POG pre-spot on dry side before cleaning cycle. GreenEarth preferred for silk — rated A, preserves hand-feel and luster. Hydrocarbon also excellent (rated A). PERC acceptable but rated C for silk — risk of odor and hand-feel change.
